title: 第 100 節
en: 1 This is one of the earliest, if not the earliest mention of the ya^-fiopavita, the sacred cord as worn over the left shoulder for sacrificial purposes; cf. Taitt. Brahm. Ill, 10, 19, 12.
286 KAUSHiTAKI-UPANISHAD.
'May milk, may food go to thee' (Rv. I, 91, 18); ' That ray which the Adityas gladden.'

Having muttered these three Rik verses, he says : ' Do not increase by our breath (pra?za), by our offspring, by our cattle ; he who hates us and whom we hate, increase by his breath, by his offspring, by his cattle. Thus I turn the turn of the grod, I return the turn of Aditya V After these words, having raised the right arm (toward Soma), he lets it go again 2.
1 This refers to movements of the arm, following the moon and the sun.

2 It is extremely difficult to translate the Vedic verses which are quoted in the Upanishads. They are sometimes slightly changed on purpose (see § n), frequently turned from their original purport by the authors of the Upanishads themselves, and then again subjected to the most fanciful interpretations by the various commentators on the Upanishads. In our paragraph (§ 8) the text followed by the commentator differs from the printed text. The commentator seems to have read : Yat te susimazB hr/dayam adhi /fondramasi sritaxn, tenamrz'tatvasyejane maham pautram aghaw rudam. I have translated according to the commentator, at least up to a certain point, for, as Professor Cowell remarks, there is an undercurrent in the commentator's explanation, implying a comparison between the husband as the sun or fire, and the wife as the moon, which it would be difficult to render in an English translation. The same or a very similar verse occurs in § 10, while other modifications of it may be seen in Ajval. Gr/hya-sutras I, 13, 7, and elsewhere. The translation of the verses in full, of three of which the Upanishad gives the beginnings only, would be according to the commentator : ' (O goddess of the moon) who hast obtained immortal joy through that which is a beautiful (portion of the sun) placed in the moon, and filling thy heart (with pleasure), may I never weep for misfortune concerning my children.'

Rv. I, 91, 16; IX, 31, 4. 'O goddess of the moon, increase! may the vigour from everywhere (from every limb of the fire or the sun) go to thee! Help us in the attainment of food.' Rv. I, 91, 18. 'O goddess of the moon, may the streams of thy milk go well to our sons, those streams of milk which are invigorating, and II ADHYAYA, IO. 287 9. Then (thirdly) let him worship on the day of the full moon the moon as it is seen in the east in the same manner, saying : ' Thou art Soma, the king, the wise, the five-mouthed, the lord of creatures. The Brahma^a is one of thy mouths ; with that mouth thou eatest the kings (Kshatriyas) ; make me an eater of food by that mouth ! The king is one of thy mouths ; with that mouth thou eatest the people (Vai^yas) ; make me an eater of food by that mouth ! The hawk is one of thy mouths ; with that mouth thou eatest the birds ; make me an eater of food by that mouth ! Fire is one of thy mouths ; with that mouth thou eatest this world ; make me an eater of food by that mouth ! In thee there is the fifth mouth ; with that mouth thou eatest all beings ; make me an eater of food by that mouth ! Do not decrease by our life, by our offspring, by our cattle ; he who hates us and whom we hate, decrease by his life, by his offspring, by his cattle. Thus 1 turn the turn of the god, I return the turn of Aditya.' After these words, having raised the right arm, he lets it go again.

10. Next (having addressed these prayers to Soma) when being with his wife, let him stroke her help to conquer the enemy. O Soma-goddess, increasing for immortal happiness (for the birth of a son), do thou place the highest glory (the streams of thy milk) in the sky.' ' That ray (sushumwa) which (as a woman) the Adityas gladden, that Soma which as imperishable the imperishable Adityas drink, may the guardian of the world (Pra^apati), Br/haspati, and king Varuz/a gladden us by it.'

The translations are made by the commentator regardless of grammar and sense : yet they command a certain authority, and must be taken into account as throwing light on the latest development of Indian mysticism.
288 KAUSHlTAKI-UPANISHAD.

heart, saying : ' O fair one, who hast obtained immortal joy by that which has entered thy heart through Pra^apati, mayest thou never fall into sorrow about thy children V Her children then do not die before her.

ii. Next, if a man has been absent and returns home, let him smell (kiss) his son's head, saying : ' Thou springest from every limb, thou art born from the heart, thou, my son, art my self indeed, live thou a hundred harvests.' He gives him his name, saying : ' Be thou a stone, be thou an axe, be thou solid 2 gold ; thou, my son, art light indeed, live thou a hundred harvests.' He pronounces his name. Then he embraces him, saying : ' As Pra^apati (the lord of creatures) embraced his creatures for their welfare, thus I embrace thee,' (pronouncing his name.) Then he mutters into his right ear, saying : ' O thou, quick Maghavan, give to him' (Rv. Ill, 36, 10 3). 'O Indra, bestow the best wishes' (Rv. II, 21, 6), thus he whispers into his left ear. Let him then thrice smell (kiss) his head, saying : ' Do not cut off (the line of our race), do not suffer. Live a hundred harvests of life ; I kiss thy head, O son, with thy name.' He then thrice makes a lowing sound over his head, saying : ' I low over thee with the lowing sound of cows.'
12. Next follows the Daiva Parimara4, the dying around of the gods (the absorption of the two 1 Cf. Arvalayana Grzhya-sutras I, 13, 7.
2 Widely scattered, everywhere desired. Comm. Professor Cowell proposes unscattered, hoarded, or unconcealed.
3 The original has asme, to us, not asmai, to him.
4 Cf. Taitt. Up. Ill, 10, 4; Ait. Brahm. V, 28; Colebrooke, Miscellaneous Essays (1873), II, p. 39.

II ADHYAVA, 13. 289 classes of gods, mentioned before, into prawa or Brahman). This Brahman shines forth indeed when the fire burns, and it dies when it burns not. Its splendour goes to the sun alone, the life (pra;za, the moving principle) to the air.
This Brahman shines forth indeed when the sun is seen, and it dies when it is not seen. Its splendour goes to the moon alone, the life (pra^a) to the air.
This Brahman shines forth indeed when the moon is seen, and it dies when it is not seen. Its splendour goes to the lightning alone, its life (pra^a) to the air.
This Brahman shines forth indeed when the lightning flashes, and it dies when it flashes not. Its splendour goes to the air, and the life (pra/za) to the air.

Thus all these deities (i.e. fire, sun, moon, lightning), having entered the air, though dead, do not vanish ; and out of the very air they rise again. So much with reference to the deities (mythological). Now then with reference to the body (physiological).
13. This Brahman shines forth indeed when one speaks with speech, and it dies when one does not speak. His splendour goes to the eye alone, the life (pra^a) to breath (pra^a).
This Brahman shines forth indeed when one sees with the eye, and it dies when one does not see. Its splendour goes to the ear alone, the life (pra^a) to breath (pra/za).
This Brahman shines forth indeed when one hears with the ear, and it dies when one does not hear. Its splendour goes to the mind aolne, the life (pra;za) to breath (pra.7za).
[3] u 29O . KAUSHlTAKI-UPANISHAD.
This Brahman shines forth indeed when one thinks with the mind, and it dies when one does not think. Its splendour goes to the breath (prawa) alone, and the life (pra?za) to breath (pra^a).

Thus all these deities (the senses, &c), having entered breath or life (pra/za) alone, though dead, do not vanish ; and out of very breath (pra;m) they rise again. And if two mountains, the southern and northern, were to move forward trying to* crush him who knows this, they would not crush him. But those who hate him and those whom he hates, they die around him.

14. Next follows the Ni^reyasadana1 (the accepting of the pre-eminence of pra/za (breath or life) by the other gods). The deities (speech, eye, ear, mind), contending with each for who was the best, went out of this body, and the body lay without breathing, withered, like a log of wood. Then speech went into it, but speaking by speech, it lay still. Then the eye went into it, but speaking by speech, and seeing by the eye, it lay still. Then the ear went into it, but speaking by speech, seeing by the eye, hearing by the ear, it lay still. Then mind went into it, but speaking by speech, seeing by the eye, hearing by the ear, thinking by the mind, it lay still. Then breath (pra;za, life) went into it, and thence it rose at once. All these deities, having recognised the pre-eminence in pra;za, and having comprehended pra/za alone as the conscious self (pra^fiatman)2, went out of this body with all these (five different kinds of 1 For other versions of this story see Kh. Up. V, 1, note 2 ; Ait. Ar. II, 1, 4, 9; Bn'h. Ar. VI, 1, 1-14; and Kaush. Up. Ill, 3.
2 Cf. Kh. Up. VII, 15, note.

II ADHYAYA, I 5. 29 I pra/m), and resting in the air (knowing that pra^a had entered the air), and merged in the ether (aka^a), they went to heaven. And in the same manner he who knows this, having recognised the pre-eminence in pra^a, and having comprehended pra;/a alone as the conscious self (pra^natman), goes out of this body with all these (does no longer believe in this body), and resting in the air, and merged in the ether, he goes to heaven, he goes to where those gods (speech, &c.) are. And having reached this he, who knows this, becomes immortal with that immortality which those gods enjoy.
zh: 這是對於雅祖波帕尼塔（ya^-fiopavita）最早的、如果不是最早的記載之一，該聖繩係為祭祀目的而穿戴於左肩；cf. Taitt. Brahm. Ill, 10, 19, 12。
286 考希塔基奧義書。
「願乳汁、願食物歸於你」（梨俱吠陀 I, 91, 18）；「那阿迪提耶神族所歡欣的光線」。
他低聲誦唸這三首梨俱吠陀詩句後，說道：「願我們的氣息（prāṇa）、我們的後嗣、我們的牲畜不增加；願憎恨我們、為我們所憎恨的人，其氣息、其後嗣、其牲畜增加。如此我轉回了提婆的輪轉，我歸還了阿迪提亞的輪轉。」說完這些話後，他舉起右臂（朝向蘇摩），然後再放下。
這是指手臂的動作，追隨月亮與太陽的運行。

將吠陀經文譯成英文或任何現代語言極其困難，尤其是在奧義書中被引用的那些經文。它們有時被刻意略加改動（見第 n 節），常被奧義書的作者本身從原本的含義中曲解，隨後又遭到各種奧義書注疏者最為任意妄為的詮釋。在我們的段落（第 8 節）中，注疏者所依據的文本與印刷本不同。注疏者似乎讀的是：*Yat te susimazB hr/dayam adhi /fondramasi sritaxn, tenamrz'tatvasyejane maham pautram aghaw rudam*。我根據注疏者的意思進行了翻譯，至少在某個程度上如此，因為如柯威爾教授（Professor Cowell）所指出的，注疏者的解釋中暗含著一股潛流，即暗示丈夫如太陽或火，妻子如月亮之間的比較，這在英文翻譯中將難以表達。同樣或極為相似的經文出現在第 10 節中，而其他變體可見於《阿爾瓦爾‧格里哈亞經》（*Ajval. Gr/hya-sutras*）I, 13, 7 及其他處。按注疏者的說法，將三個僅由奧義書給出其起始部分的經文之全文翻譯如下：「（月亮女神啊）汝已透過置於月亮中那美好的（部分）而獲得不朽之喜悅，並充滿汝心（以歡樂），願我永遠不為我之子女的不幸而哭泣。」

Rv. I, 91, 16；IX, 31, 4。「月亮女神啊，增長吧！願四面八方的活力（來自火或太陽的每一肢體）歸向於妳！幫助我們獲得食物。」Rv. I, 91, 18。「月亮女神啊，願妳乳流的溪水流向我們的兒子們，那些充滿活力的乳流，而且 第二篇章，十節。287 9. 其次（第三）讓他在滿月之日於東方看見月亮之處，以同樣的方式禮拜月亮，說道：「汝為蘇摩（Soma），國王，智者，五張之口，生物之主。婆羅門（Brahmana）是汝之一口；以此口汝食國王們（剎帝利）；以此口使我成為食物之食者！國王是汝之一口；以此口汝食人民（吠舍）；以此口使我成為食物之食者！鷹是汝之一口；以此口汝食諸鳥；以此口使我成為食物之食者！火是汝之一口；以此口汝食此世界；以此口使我成為食物之食者！在汝中有第五張口；以此口汝食一切眾生；以此口使我成為食物之食者！勿因我們的生命、我們的子嗣、我們的牲畜而衰減；恨我們者及我們所恨者，以其生命、其子嗣、其牲畜而衰減。如此我轉神之輪轉，我歸還阿迪提亞（Aditya）之輪轉。」說完這些話後，他舉起右臂，隨後放下。
10. 其次（向蘇摩獻上這些祈禱後），當他與妻子在一起時，讓他撫摸她以幫助征服敵人。噢蘇摩女神，為了不朽的幸福而增長（為了兒子的誕生），願妳將最高的榮耀（妳乳汁的溪流）置於天空。』『那道光線（sushumna）──阿迪提耶斯以女人之身所歡欣的，蘇摩以不朽之身為不朽的阿迪提耶斯所飲──願世界的守護者（Prajāpati）、婆利訶斯帕蒂與瓦魯那王藉此使我們歡欣。』
這些譯文是由注疏家不顧文法與意義而作成的；然而它們卻具有某種權威性，必須加以考慮，因為它們足以闡明印度神祕主義的最新發展。
288 考希達基奧義書（KAUSHITAKI-UPANISHAD）
心，說：「哦美麗者，汝已藉著那進入汝心之物而由原人主（Prajāpati）獲得不朽之喜悅，願汝對汝之子女永不陷入悲愁！」她的子女便不會在她之前死去。

ii. 其次，如果一個人外出歸來，當回家時，應聞（親吻）其子之頭，言道：「汝從每一肢體而生，汝生於心臟，汝，吾子，實為吾之自身，願汝活百個豐收。」他給予他名字，言說：「願汝成為石頭，願汝成為斧頭，願汝堅實如黃金；汝，吾子，實為光明，願汝活百個豐收。」他發音其名字。其後他擁抱他，言說：「如普拉闍帕蒂（生物之主）擁抱其生物以求其福祉，如是吾擁抱汝」，（發音其名字。）其後他在其右耳低聲誦說：「嗚呼，迅捷之馬格萬，賜予他」（梨俱吠陀‧第三卷‧第36讚歌‧第10頌）。「嗚呼，因陀羅，賜予最善之願望」（梨俱吠陀‧第二卷‧第21讚歌‧第6頌），如此他在其左耳低聲言說。然其後應三次聞（親吻）其頭，言說：「勿斷絕（吾族之血脈），勿令其苦。活百個豐收之生命；吾親吻汝之頭，吾子，以汝之名。」他其後三次在其頭上發出低鳴之聲，言說：「吾以牛之低鳴聲在汝上低鳴。」
12. 其次是《提婆帕里瑪拉》（Daiva Parimara），諸神的衰亡（兩者的吸收）¹ ¹ 參見《雅瓦拉耶那·格利亞經》（Arvalayana Grzhya-sutras）I, 13, 7。
2 到處散佈，無處不受欲求。注：考威爾教授提議改作「未散佈」、「囤積」或「未隱蔽」。
原文作 asme（給我們），而非 asmai（給他）。
4 參見《泰帝利亞奧義書》(Taitt. Up.) III, 10, 4；《愛迪帝亞梵書》(Ait. Brahm.) V, 28；柯爾布魯克《雜集論文》(Colebrooke, Miscellaneous Essays, 1873)，第二卷，第39頁。
II ADHYAVA, 13. 前面提及的289位提婆，融合為 pra 或梵天（Brahman）。當火焰燃燒時，梵天確實閃耀發光，當火焰熄滅時，梵天亦隨之消亡。其光輝單獨投向太陽，而生命力（pra;za，運動原理）則投向大氣。
這梵天確實在太陽被看見時閃耀而出，在看不見時便消亡。它的光輝單獨傳向月亮，其生命氣（prāṇa）則傳向空氣。
此婆羅門確實在月亮被看見時閃耀而出，在月亮看不見時則消亡。其光輝唯獨歸於閃電，其生命（pra^a）歸於空氣。
梵天確實在閃電閃耀時光芒四射，在閃電不閃耀時則消隱。其光輝進入虛空，生命（prana）亦進入虛空。
因此，所有這些提婆（即火、太陽、月亮、閃電），雖然進入了空氣中並且已然消亡，卻不會完全消散；它們自虛空中再度升起。關於提婆的部分（神話學意義）如此說明。現在談及身體的部分（生理學意義）。
13. 當人們用言語說話時，婆羅門（Brahman）確實閃耀發光；當人們不說話時，它就消亡。它的光輝只流向眼睛，生命氣（pra^a）流向呼吸（pra^a）。
當人以眼睛觀看時，梵天確實閃耀發光；當人不觀看時，梵天就消亡。它的光輝單獨流向耳朵，生命氣（prāṇa）流向呼吸（prāṇa）。
當人用耳朵聆聽時，這梵天（Brahman）確實閃閃發光，而當人不聆聽時，它就消亡了。它的光輝只傳向心靈，生命力（prāṇa）傳向呼吸（prāṇa）。
[3] u 290 · 考希塔基奧義書（Kausitaki-Upanishad）。
當人以心智思想時，梵天（Brahman）確實閃耀光輝；當人不思想時，它便消逝。其光彩唯獨流向氣息（prawa），生命（prāṇa）亦流向氣息（prāṇa）。
因此，所有這些提婆（感官等）既已進入生命氣息（prāṇa）之中，雖然已死，卻不會消亡；他們從那生命氣息本身再度興起。倘若南北兩山向前移動，試圖壓碎懂得此理者，它們也不能壓碎他。但那些恨他的人與那些他所恨的人，他們都會死在他身邊。

諸提婆（言語、眼睛、耳朵、心識）相互爭論誰最優越，便離開了這個身體，身體於是失去了生命氣，萎縮如同一根木頭。然後言語進入了它，但即使言語說話，身體仍然靜止不動。然後眼睛進入了它，言語說話，眼睛看見，身體仍然靜止不動。然後耳朵進入了它，言語說話，眼睛看見，耳朵聽聞，身體仍然靜止不動。然後心識進入了它，言語說話，眼睛看見，耳朵聽聞，心識思考，身體仍然靜止不動。然後生命氣（prāṇa，生命）進入了它，身體於是立刻甦醒了。所有這些提婆既認識到了生命氣的卓越性，並且唯將生命氣領悟為有意識的自我（prāṇātman），便帶著所有這些（五種不同的1）離開了這個身體 > 1 關於此故事的其他版本，見《香達奧義書》（Kh. Up.）V, 1，註2；《艾達瑞耶書》（Ait. Ar.）II, 1, 4, 9；《大阿蘭耶卡書》（Bṛh. Ar.）VI, 1, 1-14；及《考什塔基奧義書》（Kaush. Up.）III, 3。 > > 2 見上文第5節。
2 參照《歌者奧義書》(Kh. Up.) VII, 15，註釋。
II ADHYAYA, I 5. 29 知曉此者，既已認識普拉那（prāṇa）之卓越性，並將普拉那本身理解為有覺知之自我（prāṇātman），乃脫離此身軀（不復執著此身軀），安住於虛空之中（知普拉那已進入虛空），並融合於乙太（ākāśa），遂往天界而去。其方式與上述諸提婆（言語等）之去向相同。既已達此境地，知曉此者遂與彼諸提婆之不死性合為一體，而成就不朽，享有諸提婆所享有之不死性。
